dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 for unconscious call at [CITY] Multi-Level Business.
Hi, I'm Jordan, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She had a seizure when she came in for work. I was there.
Can you tell me your name?
Rachel… Rachel Miller.
What county are we in?
[CITY] County.
Maybe you could squeeze my finger for me?
Yeah, of course.
Airway is patent; do you have any trouble breathing?
No, I breathe fine.
Breath sounds are clear, and respirations are 18 and unlabored.
You're breathing fine. Let me check your pulse.
Pulse is 72 and regular.
Blood pressure is 131 over 91.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Let's check your blood sugar.
Blood sugar is 125 before we placed the monitors.
Can you describe what happened before the seizure?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to event
dialogue.utterance: I felt light-headed, had heart palpitations, then lost it for a minute.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to event
dialogue.utterance: Did anyone witness this?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to event
dialogue.utterance: I did. She fell directly to the floor; didn't hit her head I think.
Have you ever had seizures before?
Yes. In September I had a big one that brought me to cardiac arrest.
What medications are you currently taking?
Ativan and Celexa.
Do you have any known allergies?
I can't take tramadol, Reglan, or penicillin.
Her seizure was grand mal, looked like full convulsions.
Pain on a scale of 0 to 10?
No pain at all.
Skin is warm, dry, and intact with no rashes or bruises noted.
Can you look back and forth at my finger? You appear alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Yeah.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: Your nose and throat are unremarkable with no drainage or obstruction.
Let's check your chest. Take a deep breath. Lungs are clear, and chest is unremarkable with a normal heart rhythm.
Let's check the abdomen for tenderness or pain.
No pain up there.
I'm going to touch your arms and legs; you might feel some pressure. Extremities have normal movement and sensation with no deformities noted.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation with no deficits noted, indicating a GCS of 15.
I'm going to set up an IV. It might be a bit cold when I place it.
Attempted IV access in the arm, but it was unsuccessful after one attempt as noted in the report.
I don't need to give any medication right now, but I am starting a four-lead ECG as part of standard monitoring.
Monitor shows a paced rhythm since the ECG was successfully placed at 07:13 per the report.
SpO2 still 99%, no change there.
BP down to 116 over 82, small drop, but no concern at this time.
Pulse is still regular at 73, as recorded in the second set of vitals from the report.
Respiratory rate is now 16 breaths per minute. That's unlabored and consistent with later observations on record.
Let me check your blood sugar again; it was noted as 0 after the second set of vital signs in the report.
Mrs. Miller, would you like to be transported to [COMPANY_NAME] for further care?
Patient secured on the stretcher with a five-point safety harness and side rails as confirmed in the narrative.
Stable vital signs noted including a normal respiratory rate of 16.
Our care was guided by protocol 3-12 for medical-seizure as documented in the ePCR for field management.
[COMPANY_NAME] was chosen for their expertise in critical care based on patient needs and location as noted in the narrative.
Full patient care report, including ECG monitoring and IV attempt, has been prepared for the ER team upon arrival according to protocol.
EKG remains consistent. No interventions required at this time with the patient stable per protocol.
Patient is alert, oriented to all four parameters (A&Ox4), with stable vitals and no new symptoms noted yet with ongoing reassessment completed.
EKG rhythm remains consistent with paced pattern as shown on the monitor throughout transport per the ePCR.
Respirations still at 16 and unlabored, per the most recent reassessments documented in the ePCR report.
What was your treatment after your last seizure leading to cardiac arrest?
I had a pacemaker defibrillator implanted following that event according to the ePCR narrative.
Thanks. Are there any other ongoing issues since that experience listed in your medical records or reported by you today?
My anxiety is managed with Celexa, which is one of the medications I regularly take as noted in the list under medications in the ePCR narrative.
Vitals and patient mental status remain stable throughout the transport as previously observed, with no significant changes recorded at this time.
All transportation preparations complete for a secure and safe arrival, including patient placement and fastening in the main stretcher with five-point restraint as detailed in the narrative.
Patient belongings are with the patient in the hospital as confirmed during the hospital clearance per the ePCR narrative closure report.
Pupils are still PERRL and reactive with no noticeable changes in appearance or function from initial or subsequent checks per ePCR documentation.
No changes in condition have occurred, confirming that [COMPANY_NAME] remains the appropriate facility for this transport per standing protocol and assessment per the ePCR.
All care steps including ECG, IV attempt, and protocol followed are detailed in our report for the ER team at [COMPANY_NAME] as the final step in transport planning as outlined in the ePCR.
Rechecking oxygen levels, still 99% on room air with no intervention or concern noted for a drop in oxygen saturation, as consistently recorded throughout the encounter, as shown in the ePCR report.
